#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# changed-py-source.sh — changed Python *source* files for PR-scoped lanes.
#
# Thin wrapper over changed-files.sh that drops the non-source paths the Python
# analysis tools skip during a full-scan directory traversal (tests, scripts,
# migrations, alembic, conftest.py, setup.py).
#
# WHY: when a lane passes explicit file paths to a tool, the tool's own
# directory-scan excludes are bypassed — e.g. `interrogate -c pyproject.toml
# path/to/test_x.py` checks that file even though pyproject's
# [tool.interrogate].exclude lists "**/tests/**". That makes a PR's verdict
# diverge from the equivalent full scan (a changed test file tanks docstring
# coverage). Filtering the file list here restores parity.
#
# Mirrors changed-files.sh's three-state contract exactly so callers are
# unchanged apart from the script name:
#   __FULL__  → push / full-scan event (passed through untouched)
#   (empty)   → PR with no matching source files (caller skips & passes)
#   <list>    → PR: newline-separated source files
#
# NOTE: ruff is intentionally NOT a consumer — its full-scan command lints the
# whole tree including tests, so raw changed-files.sh py is the correct scope
# there. mypy resolves to project roots, so it is unaffected too.
set -euo pipefail

FILES=$(scripts/ci/changed-files.sh py)

# Pass the full-scan sentinel and the empty (skip) case through unchanged.
if [[ "$FILES" == "__FULL__" || -z "$FILES" ]]; then
  printf '%s\n' "$FILES"
  exit 0
fi

# Keep in sync with [tool.interrogate].exclude in pyproject.toml and the
# xenon -e / bandit path selection in code-quality.yml. Each pattern matches a
# whole path segment (anchored to start-of-string or a slash) so substrings
# like "latest_tests" or "scripts_helper.py" are not falsely excluded.
printf '%s\n' "$FILES" \
  | { grep -vE '(^|/)(tests|scripts|migrations|alembic)/|(^|/)(conftest|setup)\.py$' || true; }

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# changed-py-source.sh — changed Python *source* files for PR-scoped lanes.
#
# Thin wrapper over changed-files.sh that drops the non-source paths the Python
# analysis tools skip during a full-scan directory traversal (tests, scripts,
# migrations, alembic, conftest.py, setup.py, virtualenvs).
#
# WHY: when a lane passes explicit file paths to a tool, the tool's own
# directory-scan excludes are bypassed — e.g. `interrogate -c pyproject.toml
# path/to/test_x.py` checks that file even though pyproject's
# [tool.interrogate].exclude lists "**/tests/**". That makes a PR's verdict
# diverge from the equivalent full scan (a changed test file tanks docstring
# coverage). Filtering the file list here restores parity.
#
# The exclude list is read straight from [tool.interrogate].exclude in
# pyproject.toml — that is the single source of truth, so there is nothing to
# keep in sync by hand. interrogate is the strictest consumer; xenon and bandit
# ride on the same list (a safe superset of their own narrower excludes).
#
# Mirrors changed-files.sh's three-state contract exactly so callers are
# unchanged apart from the script name:
#   __FULL__  → push / full-scan event (passed through untouched)
#   (empty)   → PR with no matching source files (caller skips & passes)
#   <list>    → PR: newline-separated source files
#
# NOTE: ruff is intentionally NOT a consumer — its full-scan command lints the
# whole tree including tests, so raw changed-files.sh py is the correct scope
# there. mypy resolves to project roots, so it is unaffected too.
set -euo pipefail

FILES=$(scripts/ci/changed-files.sh py)

# Pass the full-scan sentinel and the empty (skip) case through unchanged.
if [[ "$FILES" == "__FULL__" || -z "$FILES" ]]; then
  printf '%s\n' "$FILES"
  exit 0
fi

# Drop the excluded paths, deriving the patterns from pyproject so this never
# drifts from interrogate's own config. Every current pattern reduces to either
# a path *segment* (**/tests, **/scripts, .venv, …) or a *basename*
# (**/conftest.py, **/setup.py); anything with a shape we don't model is left
# in (errs toward a louder lane, never a silent drop). tomllib is stdlib on the
# runner's Python 3.11+.
# The program goes via `-c` (not stdin) so the changed-file list can be piped
# in on stdin without the two colliding.
printf '%s\n' "$FILES" | python3 -c '
import pathlib
import sys
import tomllib

patterns = tomllib.loads(pathlib.Path("pyproject.toml").read_text())[
    "tool"
]["interrogate"]["exclude"]

segments, basenames = set(), set()
for pat in patterns:
    core = pat.strip("/")
    if core.startswith("**/"):
        core = core[3:]
    if core.endswith("/**"):
        core = core[:-3]
    core = core.strip("/")
    if "/" in core or "*" in core:  # shape we do not model -> do not exclude
        continue
    (basenames if core.endswith(".py") else segments).add(core)

for line in filter(None, sys.stdin.read().splitlines()):
    parts = line.split("/")
    if segments & set(parts) or parts[-1] in basenames:
        continue
    print(line)
'

content.text:
Done — #707 is now: pyproject-driven (single source of truth, nothing to hand-sync), comments stripped to essentials, the stdin-collision bug fixed, and tested. Pushed; CI will re-run (it's a strict subset of the earlier green run).

## Why this is even needed — plain version

**The speed trick:** On a PR, running every quality check against the *entire* codebase is slow. So the CI was changed (#704) to only check **the files you actually touched in that PR**. It does `git diff` to get the list of changed `.py` files and hands that list to each tool.

**The docstring checker (`interrogate`):** Its job is "≥80% of functions must have a docstring." Its config says *"don't count test files"* — you don't want to be forced to write docstrings on every test. That works fine normally.

**The bug:** That "skip test files" rule only kicks in when `interrogate` is **crawling directories itself**. When CI instead hands it an **explicit list of files** (the speed trick), `interrogate` checks *every file in the list* — it does **not** apply its own "skip tests" rule to files you named directly. (I confirmed this live: a test file scored 17.9% instead of being skipped.)

**So what happened on #688:** your PR changed some test files. CI handed those test files to `interrogate`. Test files have no docstrings → coverage got dragged down to 63% → the lane failed. But it was a *false* failure: if it had scanned the whole repo, it would have skipped those tests and passed. Same latent trap exists for `xenon` (complexity) and `bandit` (security), which also have "skip tests/scripts" rules.

**The fix (`changed-py-source.sh`):** before handing the file list to those tools, remove the files the config says to skip (tests, scripts, migrations, etc.). Now the fast per-PR check gives the same verdict the full scan would. And it reads that skip-list from `pyproject.toml` directly, so if someone changes which folders are excluded, the script follows automatically.

You already dodged this in #688 by writing docstrings on your test files — this just stops it from biting the next person. Up to you whether to merge it.
